Chromosome 3 QTL Peak — Multi-Evidence Analysis (v3)

Pararge aegeria — Haplotype-level introgression mapping using phased genotypes

🧬 v3: Phased Haplotype Analysis

This analysis uses the phased VCF from the EHH Haplotype-Based Selection Analysis (SHAPEIT4 phasing). Unlike v2 (unphased diploid genotypes), v3 resolves each individual into two separate haplotypes, enabling:

  • Haplotype-level introgression tract mapping (164 haplotypes vs 82 diploids)
  • Classification of DD individuals into 0, 1, or 2 introgressed haplotypes
  • More precise breakpoint localisation at haplotype boundaries
  • Detection of introgressed haplotypes leaking into NN population

Input: 15,618 phased biallelic SNPs in chr3: 9.5–13.0 Mb (vs 25,679 unphased in v2).

Samples Excluded

2 sample(s) removed: nr59, nr60 (same as v2; high missingness in focal region)

Summary

NN samples / haplotypes40 / 80
DD samples / haplotypes42 / 84

Max −log₁₀(p)48.1
SNPs with −log₁₀(p) > 40767
SNPs with −log₁₀(p) > 500
DD: 2 introgressed haps42 / 42
DD: 1 introgressed hap0 / 42

Approach 1: Multi-Statistic Sliding Windows

Same statistics, phased allele counts

Δ(SNP-index), FST, and Fisher's exact test now computed from phased haplotype allele counts (2N alleles per group). Smoothed with 25 kb and 50 kb windows. Phasing does not change allele frequencies or population-level statistics, but ensures consistent allele counting across individuals. The Fisher exact test −log10(p) is shown in separate panels for the 25 kb and 50 kb windows to allow detailed comparison of the peak structure at different smoothing scales.

Normalised Multi-Evidence Overlay

Approach 2: Haplotype-Level Introgression Tracts NEW in v3

Key advance over v2: individual haplotype resolution

Each individual contributes two haplotypes (2×82 = 164 total). The NN consensus allele was determined per SNP from all 80 NN haplotypes. Per-haplotype discordance was computed in 50 kb sliding windows (10 kb step). Introgression = contiguous run of >50% discordance. This resolves heterozygous individuals into one introgressed + one non-introgressed haplotype.

Reading the Tract Plot

Each individual is shown as two horizontal rows (one per phased haplotype). The plot is divided by a black line:

  • Red blocks (top half) = DD haplotypes that are discordant from the NN consensus — these are the introgressed tracts. A red block means that haplotype carries alleles that differ from the typical NN background, indicating it derives from a different ancestral source (the introgressed lineage).
  • Blue blocks (bottom half) = NN haplotypes that show discordance from their own consensus. In a clean system these should be mostly absent. Any blue blocks would indicate NN individuals carrying fragments of the introgressed haplotype.
  • Light pink/blue backgrounds = the full extent of the analysed region. Where no coloured block is drawn, the haplotype matches the NN consensus (i.e., it is not introgressed).
  • Width of each block = the physical extent of the introgression tract in Mb. Wider blocks indicate longer runs of introgressed DNA. The edges of each block are recombination breakpoints where the ancestry switches from introgressed to non-introgressed.

Step-by-Step Algorithm

The algorithm is a simplified form of local ancestry inference that leverages the two-population design (NN = reference, DD = target):

Step 1 — Build the NN consensus haplotype (reference).
At each SNP position, the modal allele across all 80 NN haplotypes is determined. For biallelic SNPs, this is equivalent to rounding the mean allele value: if >50% of NN haplotypes carry the ALT allele, the consensus is ALT; otherwise REF. This produces a single “typical NN” haplotype vector of length 15,618 SNPs.

Step 2 — Compute per-haplotype discordance in sliding windows.
For every individual (NN and DD alike), each of their two phased haplotypes is compared to the NN consensus. Within a 50 kb sliding window (stepped every 10 kb), the discordance rate is calculated as the fraction of non-missing SNPs where the haplotype allele differs from the consensus. A discordance of 0.0 means perfect match to the NN consensus; a discordance of 1.0 means every allele differs.

Step 3 — Call introgression tracts.
A haplotype is classified as introgressed in a given window if discordance > 0.50 (i.e., more alleles differ from the NN consensus than match it). Contiguous runs of introgressed windows are merged into introgression tracts, each defined by a start and end position in Mb.

Step 4 — Compute coverage and define core regions.
At each window, the fraction of DD haplotypes that are introgressed is tallied. A core introgression region is a contiguous stretch where ≥90% of DD haplotypes are introgressed. These are the regions most likely to contain the causal variant(s).

Code Used

The following Python code implements the haplotype-level tract mapping. The full script is generate_v3_report.py.

# === Step 1: NN consensus haplotype ===
# For each SNP, determine the modal allele across all NN haplotypes
nn_haps = hap_matrix[good_snps][:, :n_nn, :]      # shape: (n_snps, n_nn, 2)
nn_haps_flat = nn_haps.reshape(n_good, -1)          # flatten to (n_snps, n_nn*2)
nn_consensus = np.zeros(n_good)
for i in range(n_good):
    vals = nn_haps_flat[i]
    vals = vals[~np.isnan(vals)]
    if len(vals) > 0:
        nn_consensus[i] = np.round(np.mean(vals))   # mode ~ round(mean) for biallelic

# === Step 2: Sliding-window discordance per haplotype ===
win_size = 50000                                     # 50 kb windows
win_step = 10000                                     # 10 kb step
win_starts = np.arange(REGION_START, REGION_END - win_size + 1, win_step)

hap_disc = np.full((n_wins, n_all, 2), np.nan)      # discordance matrix
for wi in range(n_wins):
    ws, we = win_starts[wi], win_starts[wi] + win_size
    mask = (good_pos >= ws) & (good_pos < we)
    if np.sum(mask) < 3:
        continue
    cons = nn_consensus[mask]                        # consensus alleles in window
    for si in range(n_all):
        for hi in range(2):                       # each haplotype
            h = hap_good[mask, si, hi]
            valid = ~np.isnan(h)
            if np.sum(valid) > 0:
                hap_disc[wi, si, hi] = np.mean(h[valid] != cons[valid])

# === Step 3: Call introgression tracts ===
def find_tracts(disc_vec, threshold=0.5):
    # Find contiguous runs above threshold.
    tracts = []
    in_tract = False
    for i in range(len(disc_vec)):
        if not np.isnan(disc_vec[i]) and disc_vec[i] > threshold:
            if not in_tract:
                start_i = i
                in_tract = True
        else:
            if in_tract:
                tracts.append((start_i, i - 1))
                in_tract = False
    if in_tract:
        tracts.append((start_i, len(disc_vec) - 1))
    return tracts

# === Step 4: Coverage = fraction of DD haplotypes introgressed per window ===
dd_hap_introgressed = np.zeros(n_wins)
for wi in range(n_wins):
    count = 0
    for si in range(n_nn, n_all):             # DD samples only
        for hi in range(2):
            d = hap_disc[wi, si, hi]
            if not np.isnan(d) and d > 0.5:
                count += 1
    dd_hap_introgressed[wi] = count / (n_dd * 2)    # fraction of DD haplotypes

Relationship to Published Methods

This approach is a simplified, two-population form of local ancestry inference (LAI). Several established methods use conceptually similar strategies:

MethodApproachKey similarity
ChromoPainter
Lawson et al. 2012, PLOS Genetics		 HMM-based chromosome painting; reconstructs each “recipient” haplotype as a mosaic of “donor” haplotype chunks using the Li & Stephens (2003) model. Both methods compare individual haplotypes against a reference population to identify segments of different ancestry. ChromoPainter is more sophisticated (HMM, multiple donors), while our approach uses a single consensus reference and sliding-window discordance.
Gmin
Geneva et al. 2015, PLOS ONE		 Ratio of minimum to mean between-population haplotype distance, computed in sliding windows. Designed to detect recent introgression tracts. Both use sliding-window haplotype comparison between two populations. Gmin focuses on population-level summary statistics rather than per-individual tracts.
IntroUNET
Durvasula & Sankararaman 2024, PLOS Genetics		 Deep learning (U-Net CNN) that takes a binary genotype tensor as input and predicts which alleles were introgressed, framing the problem as image segmentation. Both identify per-individual introgressed segments along the chromosome. IntroUNET uses neural networks trained on simulated data; our approach uses direct empirical comparison to an observed reference population.
HAPMIX / LAMP-LD
Price et al. 2009; Baran et al. 2012		 HMM-based local ancestry inference that assigns each segment of an admixed individual’s genome to one of two (or more) ancestral populations. Our approach is functionally equivalent to a two-population LAI with a hard threshold instead of a probabilistic model. The NN consensus serves as the “non-introgressed reference” and high discordance identifies segments from the alternative ancestry.
SparsePainter
Zhu et al. 2025, Nature Communications		 Fast haplotype-based local ancestry inference using sparse reference panels. Direct improvement on ChromoPainter with orders-of-magnitude speed gain. Both use phased haplotypes and a reference panel. SparsePainter is designed for complex multi-population scenarios; our simplified approach is tailored for the clear two-population (NN vs DD) design in this experiment.

Introgression in Lepidoptera: In the Heliconius butterfly radiation, Edelman et al. (2019, Science) found that introgression has been a major contributor of genealogical discordance, with 38% of loci genome-wide showing a history of introgression. Martin et al. (2019, PLOS Biology) showed that rates of introgression are predicted by recombination rate variation across butterfly genomes. For Pararge aegeria specifically, Pruisscher et al. (2018, Molecular Ecology) identified candidate regions for diapause induction variation including the genes period and timeless, and recent work has implicated Z-linked inheritance and repeated colonisation in shaping diapause adaptation across Scandinavian populations.

Why this simplified approach works here: In contrast to multi-population admixture scenarios (humans, Heliconius), our experimental design provides a clean two-population system where NN (non-diapause) serves as the unambiguous reference and DD (diapause) is the target population potentially carrying introgressed haplotypes. The crossing design means the ancestral population structure is known, making a simple consensus-discordance approach sufficient without requiring the full machinery of HMM-based LAI or simulation-trained neural networks. The 50% discordance threshold is conservative and biologically motivated: an introgressed haplotype should differ from the local population consensus at the majority of linked sites.

Introgression Dosage NEW in v3

How many haplotypes carry the introgression per individual?

At the top core region (10.94–11.03 Mb), each individual is classified by how many of their two haplotypes are introgressed. DD: 42 homozygous (2 haps), 0 heterozygous (1 hap), 0 non-introgressed (0 haps). NN: 0 homozygous, 0 heterozygous, 40 non-introgressed.

Approach 3: Introgression Coverage & Breakpoint Density

Haplotype-level coverage (vs diploid in v2)

Coverage = fraction of DD haplotypes (out of 84) that are introgressed at each position. Core regions = contiguous segments with ≥90% coverage. Breakpoint density shows where recombination has eroded the introgression boundaries.

Approach 4: Genotype Composition

Per-window diploid genotype class frequencies

For each 50 kb sliding window (10 kb step), this plot shows the fraction of SNP×individual observations that fall into each genotype class: 0/0 (REF homozygous), 0/1 (heterozygous), and 1/1 (ALT homozygous). The fractions are computed by pooling all SNPs within the window across all individuals in each group (NN or DD), so the denominator is n_snps_in_window × n_individuals.

Step-by-Step Algorithm

For each 50 kb window at step i:

  1. Select all SNPs with <5% missingness that fall within the window boundaries.
  2. Extract the diploid genotype sub-matrix for NN samples: shape (n_snps_in_window, n_nn). Each cell is 0, 1, or 2 (sum of two haplotype alleles).
  3. Count the fraction of cells that equal 0 (REF homo), 1 (het), or 2 (ALT homo). The remaining fraction is missing data.
  4. Repeat for DD samples.
# Genotype composition per window
for wi in range(n_wins):
    ws, we = win_starts[wi], win_starts[wi] + win_size
    mask = (good_pos >= ws) & (good_pos < we)
    # NN: pool all SNPs x individuals in this window
    nn_g = geno_good[mask][:, :n_nn]        # shape: (n_snps_in_win, n_nn)
    total = nn_g.size                         # denominator = n_snps * n_nn
    geno_comp_nn[wi, 0] = np.sum(nn_g == 0) / total   # frac REF homo
    geno_comp_nn[wi, 1] = np.sum(nn_g == 1) / total   # frac het
    geno_comp_nn[wi, 2] = np.sum(nn_g == 2) / total   # frac ALT homo

Why Genotype Composition Differs from the Haplotype Heatmap

At first glance, the genotype composition stacked plot may appear discordant with the haplotype heatmap. This is because they measure fundamentally different things:

  • Haplotype heatmap shows the raw allele identity at each SNP (REF=blue, ALT=red) for each haplotype. In the introgression region, DD haplotypes are red (carry ALT alleles) because the introgressed lineage is enriched for alternative alleles relative to the reference genome. The heatmap is allele-agnostic about which allele is “introgressed” — it simply shows REF vs ALT.
  • Genotype composition shows the pooled frequency of genotype classes across many SNPs. Even in a highly differentiated region, a mix of SNPs will be ALT-fixed in DD (showing 1/1) while others may be REF-fixed in NN (showing 0/0 in NN). The genotype composition shows the aggregate pattern, not per-SNP allele identity.
  • Key insight: The genotype composition does not track “which allele is the introgressed allele” at each SNP. Instead, it pools REF/ALT identity across all SNPs equally. Since the reference genome is neither purely NN nor purely DD, some SNPs have the “introgressed” allele as REF and others as ALT. This dilutes the genotype composition signal relative to the haplotype heatmap, which shows the raw pattern clearly.
  • The tract-based analysis (Approach 2) and the haplotype heatmap are more informative for identifying introgression, because they operate at the haplotype level and compare against the NN consensus rather than against the reference genome.

Approach 5: SparsePainter Chromosome Painting NEW

Probabilistic local ancestry inference (SparsePainter v1.3.2)

SparsePainter (Yang et al. 2025, Nature Communications 16: 2742) uses the PBWT algorithm to find sparse haplotype matches and an HMM to assign local ancestry probabilities at each SNP position. All 82 individuals (NN + DD) were painted against a two-population reference panel (NN and DD) using leave-one-out to avoid self-painting bias. For each individual at each SNP, the tool outputs P(NN ancestry) and P(DD ancestry).

Method

The SparsePainter analysis was run on the full phased chr3 VCF (121,472 SNPs, 82 individuals) with the following configuration:

  • Reference file: Combined phased VCF with all 82 samples (40 NN + 42 DD)
  • Target file: Same VCF (self-painting with automatic leave-one-out)
  • Population labels: Each sample labeled as “NN” or “DD”
  • Genetic map: Approximate 1 cM/Mb linear map
  • Leave-one-out: Automatic when reffile = targetfile (each individual excluded from its own population when being painted)
  • Recombination scaling: Fixed lambda = 8,822.55 (estimated from data)
SparsePainter \
    -reffile all_combined.vcf.gz \
    -targetfile all_combined.vcf.gz \
    -popfile popfile_2pop.txt \      # 40 NN + 42 DD labels
    -mapfile mapfile.txt \
    -namefile namefile_all.txt \
    -out twopop_painting \
    -prob -aveSNP -aveind -ncores 1

Results

GroupRegionMean P(DD ancestry)
DD individualsOutside focal (rest of chr3)0.9307
DD individualsPeak (10.5–12.0 Mb)0.9923
NN individualsOutside focal (rest of chr3)0.0943
NN individualsPeak (10.5–12.0 Mb)0.0109

Interpretation: The SparsePainter results confirm the introgression signal. At the peak region (10.5–12.0 Mb), DD individuals show 99.2% DD ancestry while NN individuals show only 1.1% DD ancestry — a clean separation. Outside the focal region, there is still genome-wide differentiation (DD show ~93% DD ancestry background, reflecting genome-wide population structure), but the focal region shows an enhanced signal above this baseline.

The three panels below show: (top) P(DD ancestry) for DD individuals along the focal region, (middle) P(DD ancestry) for NN individuals, and (bottom) the difference ΔP = P(DD|DD) − P(DD|NN). The difference plot highlights the introgression-specific signal above the genome-wide background, with the peak region showing maximal differentiation.

Phased Haplotype Heatmap NEW in v3

Individual haplotypes visible (2 rows per individual)

Each individual is represented by two rows (one per haplotype). Blue = reference allele, Red = alternative allele. The phased data reveals which specific haplotype carries the introgressed block, allowing heterozygous individuals to be clearly decomposed into one NN-like and one DD-like haplotype.

v2 vs v3 — Core Region Comparison NEW

Impact of phasing on core region boundaries

Comparison of core introgression regions identified with unphased diploid genotypes (v2) vs phased haplotypes (v3). Differences arise because haplotype-level analysis can distinguish heterozygous carriers (1 introgressed haplotype) from homozygous carriers (2 introgressed haplotypes), providing more precise boundary resolution.

Method Details

Phased VCF source: SHAPEIT4 phasing of chr3 biallelic SNPs (chr3_phased.vcf.gz from EHH analysis). 15,618 SNPs in 9.5–13.0 Mb (vs 25,679 unphased in v2; fewer because phasing requires biallelic, non-missing sites).

Genotype extraction: Phased haplotypes (0|0, 0|1, 1|0, 1|1) parsed directly from VCF GT field. Each diploid individual decomposed into two haplotypes.

Statistics: Δ(SNP-index) = AFDD − AFNN; FST via Weir & Cockerham (1984); Fisher's exact test on 2×2 allele count tables. All computed from haplotype-level allele counts.

Introgression tract mapping: NN consensus = modal allele across all 80 NN haplotypes at each SNP. Per-haplotype discordance from consensus smoothed with 50 kb windows (step 10 kb). Introgression = contiguous region with >50% discordance. Core region = contiguous segment with ≥90% of DD haplotypes introgressed.

Introgression dosage: At each core region, individuals classified by count of introgressed haplotypes (0, 1, or 2).

Excluded samples: nr59, nr60 (high missingness in focal region).

SparsePainter chromosome painting: SparsePainter v1.3.2 (Yang et al. 2025, Nature Communications) was run with both NN (N=40) and DD (N=42) as reference populations. All 82 individuals were painted as targets using the same VCF as reference, with automatic leave-one-out for unbiased self-painting. Per-SNP local ancestry probabilities P(NN) and P(DD) were computed for all 164 haplotypes and averaged per population to produce the chromosome painting plots. Recombination scaling constant lambda = 8,822.55 (estimated by SparsePainter).
